prompt:
Entrance-lane firmware shim for our leisure centre — someone 'modernised' the lane classes during a slow afternoon and now the whole thing is a brick. g++ output, complete:

    g++ -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra -Werror -o test_lanes test_lanes.cpp
    card_lane.h:11:9: error: 'int CardLane::admit(int)' marked 'override', but does not override
    card_lane.h:22:17: error: 'std::string CardLane::status()' marked 'override', but does not override
    card_lane.h:27:10: error: 'void CardLane::clear_counts()' marked 'override', but does not override
    lane.h:18:17: error: 'virtual int Lane::admit(int) &' was hidden [-Werror=overloaded-virtual=]
    card_lane.h:11:9: note:   by 'int CardLane::admit(int)'
    lane.h:26:25: error: 'virtual std::string Lane::status() const' was hidden [-Werror=overloaded-virtual=]
    card_lane.h:22:17: note:   by 'std::string CardLane::status()'
    bank.h: In function 'void decommission(Lane*)':
    bank.h:14:5: error: deleting object of polymorphic class type 'Lane' which has non-virtual destructor might cause undefined behavior [-Werror=delete-non-virtual-dtor]
    bank.h: In member function 'void LaneBank::replace(std::size_t, std::unique_ptr<Lane>)':
    bank.h:27:23: error: use of deleted function 'Lane& Lane::operator=(const Lane&)'
    lane.h:13:11: note: declared here
       13 |     Lane &operator=(const Lane &) = delete;

Three overrides that apparently override nothing, a function 'hidden' by a function with the same name, a destructor complaint from a line that just deletes a pointer like C++ has always deleted pointers, and replace() tripping over an operator= that was deliberately deleted years ago because lanes hold hardware handles and must never be copied. That deleted assignment is load-bearing, by the way — please don't 'fix' the build by un-deleting it.

Behavior contract, all pinned by the tests: plain lanes admit everyone; card lanes bounce every third swipe in a party and track the bounces; the board reads status through a const base reference; close-out zeroes every counter a lane has including the card lane's re-swipe count; replacing slot hardware means the fresh lane reports as itself afterwards, not as some chimera of old and new. `make test` must compile and pass. test_lanes.cpp, mintest.h and the Makefile stay as they are.
notes: 5C4 item 5: override/virtual triage. g++ 16.1.1, -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra -Werror (delete-non-virtual-dtor and overloaded-virtual are both in -Wall on this g++; NOTE probed: the dtor warning does NOT fire from std::unique_ptr's default_delete — system-header suppression — so the seed routes teardown through a user-code decommission(Lane*) with a raw delete). Five planted defects, all visible in one pass (complete dump in prompt, no chain reveals): (1) CardLane::admit lost the base's & ref-qualifier — override error + overloaded-virtual hidden error; (2) CardLane::status lost const — same pair; the frozen test calls status() through `const Lane &`, so re-harmonizing by REMOVING const from the base breaks the test compile, pinning the fix direction; (3) CardLane::clear_counts marked override but the base method is reset_counts — the test resets via base ref and checks bounced()==0, so deleting the derived method instead of renaming it fails behaviorally (base reset only clears passed_); (4) non-virtual ~Lane with virtual members, raw-deleted in decommission() — fix: virtual dtor; (5) LaneBank::replace assigns through the base (*lanes_[i] = *fresh) hitting the deliberately deleted copy assignment — the catalog's slicing-caught-by-deleted-copy; the prompt forbids un-deleting, and the replace test (CardLane swapped for plain Lane must report 'lane open, 0 through') fails under any slicing 'fix' since the old vtable would keep reporting 'card lane...'. Reference fix: virtual dtor, `& override` + `const override` + rename on the three derived methods, and lanes_[i] = std::move(fresh). Domain overlap note: 'gate/turnstile' words appear in ts-healthgate/go-uploadgate (API gating) — unrelated layer; no existing seed models a device-driver class hierarchy.
